note_data.title: Transport in humans
keyPoints: **Blood** consists of plasma, red blood cells, white blood cells and platelets
**Red blood cells** are biconcave, lack a nucleus and contain haemoglobin to transport oxygen
**White blood cells** have a nucleus and defend the body against disease by phagocytosis and antibody production
**Platelets** are cell fragments that help blood clotting
**Arteries** carry blood away from the heart and have thick muscular walls
**Veins** carry blood towards the heart and have valves to prevent backflow
**Capillaries** are one cell thick to allow efficient exchange of materials
The **heart** has four chambers - two atria and two ventricles with valves to prevent backflow
**Double circulation** means blood passes through the heart twice in one complete circuit
**Coronary arteries** supply the heart muscle with oxygen and glucose

definitions.definition: A blood vessel that carries blood away from the heart
A blood vessel that carries blood towards the heart
A circulatory system where blood passes through the heart twice in one complete circuit
The process by which white blood cells engulf and digest pathogens
A red protein in red blood cells that combines with oxygen to transport it around the body

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give similarities AND differences between structures
Give observable features or sequence of events without explanations

modelAnswer.question: Explain how the structure of an artery is adapted to its function. [3 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Arteries have thick muscular walls to withstand high blood pressure. They have elastic fibres that stretch and recoil to maintain blood pressure. They have a narrow lumen to maintain high blood pressure.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: thick muscular walls / withstand high pressure
Mark 2: elastic fibres / stretch and recoil

commonMistakes: Never say 'red blood cells carry carbon dioxide' — they transport mainly oxygen, CO₂ is carried in plasma
Students often confuse pulmonary artery (carries deoxygenated blood) with other arteries
Never say 'arteries carry oxygenated blood' — pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood
Students forget that capillaries connect arteries to veins, not just 'small blood vessels'
Never say 'blood flows because of the heart beating' — explain pressure differences
examinerTips: Heart structure diagrams appear frequently - practice labelling all four chambers and major vessels
Always mention 'biconcave shape' and 'no nucleus' when describing red blood cell adaptations
Use the term 'pulmonary circulation' for heart-lungs circuit and 'systemic circulation' for heart-body circuit
In Pakistan's hot climate, increased sweating affects blood volume - good context for homeostasis questions
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Heart structure showing four chambers (left and right atria, left and right ventricles), major blood vessels (aorta, pulmonary artery, pulmonary vein, vena cava), and valves. Include direction of blood flow with arrows.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (3 marks) — they asked students to explain why radioactive glucose concentration increases over time in red blood cells, requiring knowledge of facilitated diffusion and carrier proteins
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — students had to identify what additional information was needed to determine white blood cell diameter in micrometres from a microscopy image
This topic was examined in M/J 2024 P2 Q6 (4 marks) — candidates needed to explain why decreased CO2 leads to decreased oxygen release from red blood cells, testing understanding of the Bohr effect
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P2 Q2 (5 marks) — students had to complete a table naming three types of white blood cells from given descriptions of their functions and appearance

workedExample.question: Explain why radioactive glucose concentration increases over time in red blood cells. (3 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify the concentration gradient - glucose is initially at higher concentration outside the red blood cells than inside
Step 2: Explain the transport mechanism - glucose cannot pass directly through the cell membrane due to its size and polarity, so requires facilitated diffusion
Step 3: Describe the process - glucose moves via specific carrier proteins from high concentration (outside) to low concentration (inside the cell) over time
workedExample.answer: Glucose diffuses/moves from high to low concentration gradient. This occurs by facilitated diffusion as glucose cannot pass directly through the membrane. Specific carrier proteins transport glucose into the red blood cells.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for concentration gradient, 1 mark for facilitated diffusion/carrier proteins, 1 mark for direction of movement

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused active transport with facilitated diffusion when explaining glucose uptake' — always specify that glucose transport into cells is passive and requires no energy
Examiner Report 2024: 'Students frequently forgot to mention carrier proteins when describing facilitated diffusion' — always include the role of specific transport proteins in your answers

Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates often described osmosis instead of diffusion for glucose transport' — remember that glucose transport involves facilitated diffusion, not osmosis which is only for water movement
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a detailed diagram of the human heart showing four chambers (left and right atria, left and right ventricles), major blood vessels (aorta, pulmonary artery, pulmonary veins, vena cavae), and heart valves (bicuspid, tricuspid, semi-lunar valves). The diagram should include arrows showing blood flow direction and be clearly labeled. Additionally, students should be able to draw a red blood cell showing its biconcave shape and a diagram of gas exchange in the alveoli showing the relationship between alveoli, capillaries, and the direction of oxygen and carbon dioxide movement.
note_data.quickRecap: Human transport involves the circulatory system with heart, blood vessels, and blood. Heart has four chambers pumping oxygenated blood from lungs to body via arteries, and deoxygenated blood back via veins. Blood contains red blood cells (oxygen transport), white blood cells (immunity), platelets (clotting), and plasma (transport medium). Gas exchange occurs in alveoli where oxygen enters blood and carbon dioxide is removed.
